title: Chapter 34: The Extraction
content: Ash found her on the third-floor stairwell landing.

He came through the fire door at a dead run, his jacket torn at the shoulder, a gash above his eyebrow bleeding freely down his cheek. He looked like he'd been through a war. He had — his war, the distraction upstairs, whatever had happened while she was underground.
"Go," he said. No greeting. No questions. Just that single word, sharp as a command, and he was already moving — not toward the stairs, but toward the east corridor.
Wren followed.

The building was in full lockdown. Alarm klaxons split the air like sirens, the strobing red lights turning the corridors into a stutter-film of shadow and glare. Staff pressed against walls, evacuate-and-shelter protocols conflicting, no one sure whether to run or hide. Nobody noticed two invisible people sprinting through the chaos.
"The hounds are loose," Ash said over his shoulder. "Two scouts. Ground floor."
Wren's stomach dropped. She'd felt something downstairs — two dense electromagnetic signatures, denser than cameras, denser than the biometric locks. The hounds. Active and hunting.
"Can they track us?"
"Heat signature. They'll have ours from the lobby sweep."

The east corridor was clear — the evacuation had pulled everyone toward the main exits. Wren ran with Ash, matching his stride, the Signal Key a hard pulse against her ribs. The files in her bag rustled with each step. Evidence. Proof. All of it worthless if they didn't make it out.
The first Hound appeared at the corridor junction.

It came around the corner like a bullet — low, fast, mechanical legs blurring against the tile floor. Matte black. Red sensor array sweeping. The same model that had taken Petra, but this one was running hot, its processors overclocked, its targeting systems locked onto something.
Them.
"Door!" Ash shouted.

Wren didn't think. She felt the security door on their left — electromagnetic lock, engaged, closed — and she pushed. The lock screamed. Sparks showered from the frame. The door cracked open and Ash slammed his weight against it, sending it wide.
They plunged through into a corridor of offices. Behind them, the Hound's claws scraped tile as it pivoted to pursue.

Wren turned and pushed again — not at the lock this time, but at the door itself. The hinges buckled. The frame warped. The door slammed shut with the sound of a car crash, metal shrieking against metal.
It wouldn't hold. She knew that. Hounds were designed to breach.
"Move!"

They ran. Left. Right. Through an open-plan office where a terrified accountant cowered under a desk. Through a kitchen that smelled of burnt coffee and fear. Through a conference room with a wall of windows showing the city lights, blurred by rain.
The second Hound came from ahead.

It burst through a fire door, its mechanical legs throwing sparks on the tile, its sensor dome swinging toward them with the deliberateness of a weapon acquiring its target. Two of them now — one behind, one ahead. Pincer movement. Pack tactics.
Ash didn't slow down.
He met the second Hound head-on.

The collision was vicious. Ash hit the machine at full sprint, his shoulder driving into its midsection, his hands grabbing for the sensor dome. The Hound staggered — two hundred pounds of armored alloy rocked by a man who shouldn't have been able to touch it.

But Ash was Stage 4, whether he admitted it or not. His glitch-power wasn't just electromagnetic — it was kinetic somehow, amplified by the same neural fire that made him invisible. When he grabbed the Hound's head, the machine's circuits screamed.
So did Ash.

His hands clamped on the sensor array and pulled. Metal groaned. The Hound thrashed, its legs scrambling, one foreleg catching Ash across the thigh and sending him spinning. He hit the wall but kept his grip — and kept pulling.

The sensor array tore free with a sound like a gasp. Wires snapped. The Hound emitted a howl — not mechanical, but something worse, a synthesized shriek of loss that sounded almost biological. It staggered in a circle, blind now, its targeting systems shredded.
Ash dropped the sensor array and scrambled to his feet. Blood ran down his leg. Blood ran down his face. His hands were shaking and smoking.
"GO!" he roared.

Wren ran. She grabbed his arm and hauled him with her. Behind them, the blinded Hound careened off walls. The first Hound was still coming — she could hear it, feel it, a growing electromagnetic pressure at the back of her skull.
The corridor ended at an emergency exit. Crash bar. Alarm trigger. No electronic lock — just a bar and a door and the rain beyond.
Wren hit the bar with both hands.

The door flew open. Cold air and rain and the sounds of the city — horns, traffic, the white noise of ten thousand lives being lived in plain sight. She and Ash tumbled out into an alley, the rain hitting them like a slap, and the door crashed shut behind them.
The Hound hit it from the other side. Once. Twice. The door shuddered but held.
They ran.
Out of the alley. Into the street. Into the crowd.

It was late and it was raining and the sidewalks were crowded with people hurrying home, umbrellas raised, heads down. Wren and Ash ran through them like ghosts through a graveyard — present but intangible, felt but unseen. A woman lurched as Ash brushed past. A man stumbled when Wren's shoulder caught his arm. They didn't know why. They wrote it off. Wind. Clumsiness. Nothing.
But the crowd was protection. A thicket of warm bodies, heat signatures overlapping and intermingling. The Hounds couldn't isolate two signatures in a sea of thousands. The city swallowed them.
They ran for six blocks. Then seven. Then eight.

Ash slowed first. His breathing was ragged — wet, torn. The leg wound was worse than it had looked in the strobing corridor. He was limping, favoring his left side, leaving a trail of diluted blood on the wet pavement.
"Keep moving," Wren said.
"I'm moving."
"Move faster."
He grunted. But he moved faster.

They found a bus shelter and stopped, pressing themselves against the glass, invisible among the commuters waiting for the midnight routes. Wren's chest heaved. Rain ran down her face. She could taste it — city rain, chemical and metallic and real.
She pulled the Signal Key from her jacket. It still pulsed. Slower now. Calmer. As if it knew they'd made it.
Ash leaned against the shelter glass, his breath coming in sawing gasps. Blood dripped from his chin. The gash on his brow had opened wider during the run.
"We got it," he said.
"We got it."
"And the files?"
Wren touched her bag. The papers were damp but intact. The drives were waterproof. "Everything. His whole operation. Every subject."
Ash closed his eyes. Rain hit his face and he didn't flinch. "They know we're here now. The full deployment will accelerate."
"Then we accelerate too."
He opened his eyes and looked at her. In the amber glow of the shelter light, bloodied and broken, he was the most real thing she'd ever seen.
"We need to get back," she said. "Before—"
Before the Hounds adapted. Before the trail went cold. Before Petra's fate became everyone's fate.
Ash straightened. Winced. Started walking.

They moved through the rainy city like two wounds heading for cover — visible to no one, real to each other, carrying a device that could change everything and a stack of files that proved they'd never been patients at all.
They'd been inventory. All along.
And inventory was starting to escape.
